title: High Performance Browser Networking
subtitle: What Every Web Developer Needs to Know About Browser Networking
author: Ilya Grigorik
description: A practical, deep dive into the entire networking stack that connects every web application to its users. Ilya Grigorik, a performance engineer at Google and one of the leading voices behind HTTP/2 adoption, begins with the physical layers — Wi-Fi, cellular radio, and the last-mile link — then walks through TCP, TLS, DNS, and the transport protocols that carry every packet. The book systematically explains why HTTP/1.x is structurally limited, how HTTP/2's multiplexing, header compression, server push, and stream prioritization remove those limits, and why HTTP/3 and QUIC — a UDP-based transport invented at Google — are the next necessary step. The second edition (2021) brings every chapter current, adds QUIC in depth, and covers real-time transports including WebRTC and WebSocket. The final chapters connect networking to rendering: how arriving bytes turn into paint events, where reflow and repaint hide real cost, and how a developer who understands the full stack can deliver pages that feel instant.
keyIdeas: Latency is the bottleneck, not bandwidth — optimizing latency has orders of magnitude more impact than doubling throughput
TCP slow start, Nagle's algorithm, and delayed ACK interact to create hidden performance taxes in real applications
TLS adds two round trips (one with session resumption) before application data flows — every millisecond of handshake overhead compounds
HTTP/1.x is fundamentally limited by head-of-line blocking at both the connection and the browser limit
HTTP/2 multiplexing removes application-layer head-of-line blocking, but TCP head-of-line blocking at the transport layer still lurks
HTTP/3 and QUIC eliminate TCP head-of-line blocking by running over UDP with built-in reliability, encryption, and stream reordering
The browser rendering pipeline — parse, layout, paint, composite — is the invisible final stage between arriving bytes and user-perceived speed
Bandwidth is about capacity; latency is about responsiveness — for every interactive web application, responsiveness wins
keyTakeaways: Latency dominates real-world web performance far more than bandwidth; on a 100ms RTT link, arriving bytes cost more than the bitrate for any realistic payload

TCP slow start forces every TCP connection to begin at one packet — cumulative window growth makes repeated connections structurally penalized; connection reuse and preconnect are genuine optimizations
Nagle's algorithm batches small writes waiting for an ACK; delayed ACK waits up to 40ms before sending a partial ACK; together they add a time-of-death penalty to HTTP request-response pairs
TLS 1.3 with session resumption drops the handshake to one round trip and 0-RTT for resumed clients — the difference between 274ms and 50ms on first contact
HTTP/1.x's six-connections-per-origin limit and serial request pipelining are the direct cause of the 'waterfall' in browser DevTools — the problem is not bad code, it is bad protocol design
HTTP/2 multiplexing allows many concurrent logical streams over a single TCP connection, removing application-layer head-of-line blocking and enabling server push
Critical Rendering Path optimization means the browser cannot paint until CSSOM and DOM are ready — render-blocking resources are silently expensive
HTTP/3 over QUIC removes TCP head-of-line blocking entirely by assigning each stream its own loss recovery, enabling faster page load over lossy mobile links
WebRTC brings peer-to-peer data channels and media to the browser with full NAT traversal via ICE/STUN/TURN — it is not just for video calls
Performance optimization follows three steps — measure with the Navigation Timing and Resource Timing APIs, identify the bottleneck, then eliminate it systematically
whoShouldRead: Frontend engineers who want to understand why their site feels slow even when the console is clean
Backend engineers optimizing APIs, CDN caching layers, and TLS termination
DevOps and SREs managing load balancers, reverse proxies, and HTTP/3 rollout
Network engineers entering the web performance space who need browser-side framing
Tech leads and architects deciding whether to invest in HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 for their platform
Mobile developers who must reason about variable radio conditions and intermittent connectivity
